content.subtitle: Main Modals
items: The nine main modals (will, would, can, could, may, might, shall, should, must) have only one form (ie they do not change tense or person) and are followed by a simple or continuous bare infinitive (eg could + do, be doing, have done, have been doing).
They can also be followed by a bare infinitive in the passive (eg could + be done, have been done).

content.subtitle: Semi-modals
items: Semi-modals have similar meanings to modals. They include: need (to), ought to, had better and have (got) to.
Some semi-modals, such as had better, do not change tense or person. Others, such as have (got) to, do.
Some semi-modals can be used in combination with modals, producing phrases such as might have to.

content.subtitle: Current or general ability
examples: You can't really speak seven languages fluently, can you?

content.subtitle: Past ability
examples: There's no way you could read when you were two!

content.subtitle: Decisions made now about future ability
examples: I can get you a paper when I go to the shop, if you like.

content.subtitle: Future ability
examples: One day, maybe, all adults will be able to read and write.

content.subtitle: Hypothetical ability (present/future)
examples: I couldn't go on a quiz show. I'd be too scared!
I could go with them to the cinema tomorrow but I won't because I've already seen the film.

content.subtitle: Past hypothetical ability
examples: They could have asked the Prime Minister much more searching questions. I wonder why they didn't.

content.subtitle: Negative forms
content.description: The full negative form of modals is written as two words, eg could not. The exception is cannot, which is one word.

content.subtitle: Infinitives
content.description: Can and could cannot be used as infinitives. We can use to be able to instead.
examples: I'd love to be able to come with you to the cinema tomorrow but I just can't.

content.subtitle: Past ability on one occasion
content.description: We don't usually use could for past ability on one occasion. We use was/were able to, managed or succeeded, etc.
examples: Luckily, she was able to finish the article in time.

content.subtitle: Exceptions (perception verbs)
content.description: However, with verbs such as see, hear, feel, etc we can use could for past ability on one occasion.
examples: I could see that she was tired.

content.subtitle: Asking for permission
examples: Can I finish watching this before I go to bed?

content.subtitle: Giving/refusing permission
examples: No, you can't.

content.description: May is more polite and formal than could, and could is more polite and formal than can.
examples: We don't usually use modals to talk about past permission. We can use was/were allowed to: We were allowed to buy one comic each.
However, we do use could to talk about past permission in reported speech: Mum said we could buy one comic each.

content.subtitle: Asking for and giving advice
examples: You should try to get that poem published.

content.description: We can only use hadn't better in questions.
examples: Hadn't you better check that these facts are actually true?

content.description: We can also use might/may as well to give advice and make suggestions. This suggests that, although the suggestion is not perfect, there is no better option.
examples: We may as well watch this as there's nothing else on.

content.subtitle: Criticising past behaviour
examples: You shouldn't have spoken to Mrs Todd like that.

content.subtitle: Expressing annoyance at past behaviour
examples: You could/might have told me you were going to be late!

content.subtitle: Criticising general behaviour
examples: He will slam the door every time he goes out.

content.subtitle: Criticising a specific example of someone's general behaviour
examples: You would take the car just when I wanted to go out.

content.subtitle: Might as well for criticism
examples: I might as well be dead for all you care.

content.subtitle: Current or general obligation
examples: You have to be a good communicator to be a press spokesperson.

content.subtitle: Lack of current or general obligation
examples: You don't always need to have a degree to become a journalist.

content.subtitle: Future obligation
examples: You'll have to do quite a lot of research before you write this report.

content.subtitle: Lack of future obligation
examples: I'm glad we won't have to write any more essays on this course.

content.subtitle: Past obligation
examples: We had to come up with three questions each.

content.subtitle: Lack of past obligation
examples: In the past, politicians didn't have to deal with being in a 24-hour media spotlight.

sections.title: A: Circle the correct word or phrase
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: You **can't / couldn't** really talk when you were one year old, **can / could** you?
We **can / could** give you a lift home tonight, if you want.
I wish I **can / could** speak Russian, then **I'd be able to / I could** read Tolstoy in the original.
items.answer: could, I'd be able to/I could

items.sentence: Excuse me, **could / may** you tell me how to get to the leisure centre from here?
I'm afraid we **can't / won't be able to** come to the wedding after all.
Thankfully, we **could / managed to** get to the airport just in time so we didn't miss the plane.
Dawn couldn't **go / have gone** to the barbecue as she had to work.
Would you like to **could / be able to** buy the flat you're renting?
In a thousand years, **can humans / will humans be able to** travel faster than the speed of light?
Mum, can I watch TV and do my homework later? No, you **may / might not!**
We could **catch / have caught** the last train after all, but of course we didn't know that at the time.
**Mayn't / Can't** I at least stay up till the end of the film?
Everyone **could / was able to** tell that Julie was upset about something.
**May / Can I** open the window, please? It's a little stuffy in here.
Because we had guests last Saturday, the kids **could / were allowed to** stay up till midnight.

sections.title: B: Verb gap fill
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: It seems there's nothing anybody can ................. to stop the conflict escalating further.
She couldn't ................. anything in her left arm for about two hours. She said it was completely numb.
I don't think the Prime Minister will ever manage ................. from this scandal.
Can I ................. Tracy round after tea? We want to play my new computer game.
Liz has had another ice cream so why can't I ................. one? It's not fair!
When I was a child, my brothers and sisters and I weren't allowed ................. with the grown-ups except on special occasions.
Darren's allergic to wheat, so he can't ................. bread under any circumstances.
Dominic had said he spoke German, but when we got there it turned out he couldn't ................. a word!
I'd love to be able ................. from home, but in my job it's just not possible.
James could ................. for Parliament at the last election – he had enough support – but he wasn't willing to sacrifice his privacy.
A small cot for babies can ................. if you ask at reception.
We couldn't ................. to the concert anyway as Jamie wasn't very well.

sections.title: C: Error correction
sections.instructions: If the word in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Shouldn't you **getting** planning permission before you start building?
Gavin really ought not to be worn casual clothes to the interview last week.
She might have **gave** us a call to let us know she was all right.
You **should** better get a second opinion before you do anything drastic.
I don't think you **must** go to school today if you're not feeling well.
We definitely **should** not judge Karen until we know exactly what happened.
I probably won't get the job but I **should** as well apply for it, I suppose.
You **could** have told us you'd changed your mind about the holiday.
We had **rather** book the tickets now, hadn't we?
Terry **would** leave his bedroom in such a mess all the time.
Michael was late, but then again he **will** be.
You **may** have asked me if I wanted to come with you!

sections.title: D: Rewriting sentences
sections.instructions: Write a word or short phrase in each gap so that the second sentence has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
I would suggest seeing a doctor if it doesn't clear up.
You had ................. a doctor if it doesn't clear up.

It was typical of Jerry to forget to feed the cat, wasn't it?
Jerry ................. to feed the cat, ................. he?

I'm annoyed Diana didn't invite us to her engagement party.
Diana ................. us to her engagement party.

Buying a yellow car was a mistake – everyone thinks I drive a taxi!
I ................. a yellow car – everyone thinks I drive a taxi!

Teenagers are noisy, aren't they?
Teenagers ................. make so much noise, ................. they?

It won't harm us to see what special offers are on at the moment.
We ................. what special offers are on at the moment.

Don't you think you should type that essay, rather than write it in pen?
................. better type that essay, rather than write it in pen?

It would probably have been a good idea if you'd asked Dad what he thought.
You ................. to ................. Dad what he thought.

sections.title: E: Word gap fill
sections.instructions: Write a word from the box in each gap. You will need two of the words twice.

items.sentence: I ................. have bothered preparing so much food; nobody ate anything anyway.
Do you ................. to have that music on so loud? I can't concentrate!
You ................. call before you come round, as we'll definitely be in.
It was difficult, but I knew I ................. to apologise.
Trisha ................. need to get a summer job as her parents are quite well-off.
................. you have to be there in court yourself, or can a lawyer represent you?
We didn't ................. to wear a uniform to school today, so I went in jeans and a T-shirt.
I think Damien ................. to stay in hospital for the next four or five weeks.
Children ................. not be left unattended under any circumstances.
We haven't ................. to move out for another six months, but we've started looking for a new place anyway.

sections.title: F: Tabloid editor (gap fill)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: As editor of the most popular British tabloid, I need (1) ...... make sure we give our readers what they want.
It is my job to tell the readers when a celebrity has done something they should not have (2) ......
or when a politician (3) ...... to have done something but didn't.
One accusation is that we think we (4) ...... not need to worry about the truth.
I have (5) ...... be shown proof from at least two sources before I run a story.
As a newspaper, you have (6) ...... to get your facts right.
There are times when, in retrospect, we (8) ...... not have been so careful.
but we (9) ...... to have absolute proof first.
they realise they might as (10) ...... put their side of the story across.
They know they (11) ...... definitely have to speak out at some point.
celebrities need to (12) ...... talked about constantly or they stop being famous.
so they (13) ...... not complain, in my view.
you (14) ...... not fear us if you haven't done anything wrong.
you (15) ...... better look out, because we are going to catch you.

sections.title: G: Complete with must, have to, need or need to
sections.instructions: Complete using must, have to, need or need to in the correct form and the correct form of the words in brackets.

items.sentence: You ...... (pay) me back now, but of course you can if you like!
Dogs ...... (keep) on a leash at all times.
If Linda ...... (pick up) from school tomorrow, maybe we could spend the afternoon in town.
items.answer: needs to be picked up / has to be picked up

items.sentence: We ...... (rely) on phone lines in the future as we'll all have mobile phones which connect to the Internet.
items.answer: won't need to rely / won't have to rely

items.sentence: The invitations ...... (send off) soon otherwise they won't get there in time.
items.answer: need to be sent off / have to be sent off

items.sentence: Sorry I'm late. I ...... (get) a few things from the supermarket on my way here.
items.answer: had to get / needed to get

items.sentence: ...... (you / make) a fuss to get your money back, or was the manager quite reasonable?
Thankfully we ...... (believe) everything Graham tells you. He's lied to me before.
items.answer: don't need to believe / don't have to believe

items.sentence: What time ...... (farmers / get up) every morning?
items.answer: do farmers have to get up / do farmers need to get up

items.sentence: I'd hate ...... (commute) to work every morning and evening, wouldn't you?
items.answer: to have to commute / to need to commute

items.sentence: Although we took CDs with us we actually ...... (do) as there was no CD player to play them on.
items.answer: didn't need to do / didn't have to do

sections.title: H: Circle the correct word or phrase
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: 'Did you hear a noise?' '**That'll / That must** probably be the cat.'
'I had to walk all the way here.' 'You **must be / have been** exhausted.'
'Your favourite programme's on.' 'It **mustn't / can't** be. It's only half past five.'
'Andy made me redo every single one of the reports.' 'You **must / can** have been furious.'
'Laura walked right past me without saying a word.' 'She **can't / couldn't** have seen you.'
'I can't believe I got the final question wrong.' 'Don't worry. I **wouldn't / won't** have known the answer either.'
'I can't wait to get that blue top tomorrow.' 'They only had one left, so they **may / might** well have sold out.'
'It's got very cold.' 'Yes, I think it **must / might** easily snow tonight.'
'I can't wait till Roger gets here!' 'He should **land / have landed** by now, so he'll be here in about an hour.'
'I hope they've got some tickets left.' 'We **should / might** just be lucky and get the last two.'
Even in a million years, we ............... go back in time.
I ............... at the exam paper lying on Mr Gregson's desk, but I didn't want to risk it.
............... Eliot better tell his mum what happened?
You ............... have let us know you'd changed your phone number.
............... lectures at your university?
options: A Haven't students got to have attended
D Don't students have to attend

items.sentence: We ............... as well try to sell this old sofa before throwing it out.
Jeremy may well not ............... about the accident yet.
It's essential that the documents ............... be destroyed immediately.
Luckily I ............... a new pair of sunglasses as I found mine at the bottom of a bag.
I'm thinking of going on Million Euro Spender; I'd love to ............... spend a million euros in one day!
